step_by_step_thinking:
I agree with the consensus that the deciduous lower central incisor possesses the smallest mesiodistal diameter. One additional point to reinforce this is that not only do deciduous teeth tend to be overall smaller due to physiological differences between primary and permanent dentition, but also there is an inherent variation in size among individual tooth groups within each set of primary teeth. Lower central incisors fall into both categories—they are part of the anterior group of deciduous teeth which tends to be more compact, and they themselves represent the smallest members within this group. Therefore:

Answer: A **deciduous lower central incisor.**
```plaintext

Lower central incisors fall into both categories—they are part of the anterior group of deciduous teeth which tends to be more compact, and they themselves represent the smallest members within this group.
